summary: Crazy Fingers → Drums jam, tight Half‑Step into Franklin’s Tower, stellar Hell in a Bucket
blurb: The second set shines with a smoking Crazy Fingers → Drums → Space → Wheel stretch and a seamless Half‑Step into Franklin’s Tower. Jerry delivers shredding guitar and recovered vocals while Brent adds depth on keyboards. Highlights also include an iconic scream on Hell in a Bucket and a tight Friend of the Devil.
review: On October 29, 1985 the Fox Theatre in Atlanta hosted what many fans now cite as a mid‑’80s pinnacle of Dead magic. The first set opened with a crisp “Feel Like A Stranger” and “Friend Of The Devil,” but it was the second set that truly defined the night. A blistering “Mississippi Half-Step Uptown Toodeloo” launched into an inspired “Franklin’s Tower,” then flowed effortlessly through “Lost Sailor” and “Saint Of Circumstance” before exploding into a smoking “Crazy Fingers” that segued into one of the most celebrated Drums/Space/Wheel stretches of the era. The band kept the momentum rolling with “The Other One,” “Wharf Rat,” and a rousing “Johnny B. Goode,” closing the night on a heartfelt “Brokedown Palace.” Jerry’s guitar tone was described as perfect, his vocals had bounced back from a rough summer, and he was literally “shredding from the gate” during the jams. Brent Mydland asserted himself on keyboards, adding depth—even if a few listeners felt his mix sat a touch forward—while Phil Lesh laid down a rock‑solid bass foundation that powered the extended improvisations. Bob Weir’s classic scream after the bridge of “Hell In A Bucket” was a highlight for every listener, and Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming kept the groove tight and “drippy” throughout the marathon second set. Across both soundboard and audience sources the performance is consistently praised as high‑energy, tightly executed, and one of the tour’s top shows.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All recorded analyses (except one mixed) are overwhelmingly positive, repeatedly calling the show 'smoking', 'epic', and 'legendary' with multiple mentions of exceptional playing on Crazy Fingers, Half‑Step, Franklin’s Tower, and Hell In A Bucket. No consistent negative commentary appears, just a few minor technical notes, so a strong positive adjustment is justified.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with crystal‑clear mix, no dropouts, and full representation of the celebrated second‑set jam sequence.

key_highlights: Smoking Crazy Fingers → Drums → Space → Wheel stretch that defines the second set.
Seamless Half‑Step into Franklin’s Tower creating a high‑energy peak.
Bob Weir's iconic scream on Hell In A Bucket and Brent Mydland's assertive keyboards.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s vocals recovered and his guitar tone was perfect; he shredded from the gate throughout the jams, delivering strong vocal delivery even with a minor lyrical slip during Crazy Fingers.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provided rock‑solid, aggressive bass that anchored the extended improvisations, especially on The Other One.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s classic scream after the bridge of Hell In A Bucket is celebrated as a highlight and his rhythm work stayed tight throughout.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland asserted himself with deep, integral keyboard textures; praised as rock‑solid though a few noted his mix sat slightly forward on some recordings.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming was solid and ‘drippy,’ driving the Crazy Fingers jam and keeping the rhythm tight during the Drums/Space/Wheel segment.
